coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/silk fibroin/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/α-tricalcium phosphate nanocomposite electrospinned as a scaffold for wound healing application: In vitro and in vivo studies
coredata.dc:creator: Dehghani, Niloofar
Haghiralsadat, Fatemeh
Yazdian, Fatemeh
Sadeghian-Nodoushan, Fatemeh
Ghasemi, Nasrin
Mazaheri, Fahime
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Naghib, Seyed Morteza
coredata.dc:description: A highly porous nanofibrous network that can functionalize antibacterial and therapeutic agents can be considered a suitable option for skin wound healing. In this study, α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP)/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) nanocomposite was synthesized and then applied to the fabrication of novel chitosan (CS)/silk fibroin (SF)/N-CQDs/α-TCP wound dressing via electrospinning system. The prepared nanomaterials were well characterized using X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared, scanning and transmission electron microscopes analyses, and antibacterial assay. Furthermore, nanofibers were evaluated regarding their physical properties, such as tensile behavior, water uptake capacity, and water contact angle. The results reveal that CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP showed lower MIC values against E. coli and S. aureus (1.45 ± 0.26 mg/mL and 1.59 ± 0.12 mg/mL) compared to other synthesized materials. Also, in-vitro investigations were performed, and the MTT assay on the HFF cell line revealed that CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber could possess good biocompatibility. Interestingly, the scratch test proved that faster cell migration and proliferation occurred in the presence of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP 73.23 ± 2.71 %). Finally, we examined the wound healing ability of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber using an animal model. The results confirmed that produced nanofiber could efficiently promote wound closure by 96.73 ± 1.25 % in 12 days. Histopathological analyses verified accelerated re-epithelization and well-structured epidermis in CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber-treated group. Based on our findings, the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber with excellent antimicrobial properties is highly suitable for wound healing and skin tissue regeneration applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra. (B) X-ray diffraction patterns of pure α-TCP, N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP nanomaterials. (C) TEM image of the N-CQDs/α-TCP. The scale bars in the images are 200 nm (left) and 100 nm (right).
(A) SEM image, (B-E) Elemental mapping and (F) EDS spectrum of N-CQDs NPs.
(A) SEM image, (B) Elemental mapping, and (C-H) EDS spectrum of N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs.
FTIR spectra of pure chitosan, pure silk fibroin, chitosan/silk fibroin nanofiber and chitosan/silk fibroin/CQDs-TCP nanofiber.

(A, B) SEM image of chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers before and after cross-linking process. (C) Diameter distribution, (D) EDX spectrum, and (E–J) elemental mapping of the chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers.

Water contact angle measurement of electrospun mats. (A) Pictures of the water droplet on chitosan/silk fibroin and (B) chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers. (C) The summary bar graph indicates the comparison of WCA between the two mentioned scaffolds (mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05). (D) Swelling ratio, ns. indicates no significant difference (mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05). (E) Degradation behavior of chitosan/silk fibroin and chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers (mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05).

Stress-strain curves of electrospun mats (a) chitosan/silk fibroin (b) cross-linked chitosan/silk fibroin (c) chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP and (d) cross-linked chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP.
MIC values of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/α-TCP, CS/N-CQDs/α-TCP, and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP materials against standard strains of S. aureus and E. coli (mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001).

(A) In vitro cytotoxicity of CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers on HFF cell line. The two-way ANOVA test was applied. n.s. indicates no significant difference. (mean ± SD, n = 3, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001). The morphology of HFF cells in untreated (B) and treated group with CS/SF (C), and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NCs (D).

In vitro wound healing assay. Evaluation of the impact of the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers on the migration of HFF cells into the cell-free area. The summary bar graph represents the quantification of wound healing according to the scratched region percentage. The t-test analysis was used (mean ± SD, n = 3, *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001).

(A) Sequential photographs of in vivo wound healing study in rat model within 12 days. (B) Comparison of wound closure rates in different groups on days 3, 6, 9, and 12 (mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001).

Histopathology analysis of healed incisions at days 6 and 12 post-surgery stained with hematoxylin-eosin (Scale bar: 200 μm). Black sign: blood vessel, blue sign: stratum corneum, green sign: stratum basale, yellow sign: papillary dermis, red sign: fibroblast cell, orange sign: connective tissue fibers. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Mechanical properties of CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP mats before and after the cross-linking process (mean ± SD, n = 3, P < 0.05).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly porous nanofibrous network that can functionalize antibacterial and therapeutic agents can be considered a suitable option for skin wound healing. In this study, α-tricalcium phosphate (α-TCP)/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) nanocomposite was synthesized and then applied to the fabrication of novel chitosan (CS)/silk fibroin (SF)/N-CQDs/α-TCP wound dressing via electrospinning system. The prepared nanomaterials were well characterized using X-ray diffraction, Fourier-transform infrared, scanning and transmission electron microscopes analyses, and antibacterial assay. Furthermore, nanofibers were evaluated regarding their physical properties, such as tensile behavior, water uptake capacity, and water contact angle. The results reveal that CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP showed lower MIC values against E. coli and S. aureus (1.45 ± 0.26 mg/mL and 1.59 ± 0.12 mg/mL) compared to other synthesized materials. Also, in-vitro investigations were performed, and the MTT assay on the HFF cell line revealed that CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber could possess good biocompatibility. Interestingly, the scratch test proved that faster cell migration and proliferation occurred in the presence of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP 73.23 ± 2.71 %). Finally, we examined the wound healing ability of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber using an animal model. The results confirmed that produced nanofiber could efficiently promote wound closure by 96.73 ± 1.25 % in 12 days. Histopathological analyses verified accelerated re-epithelization and well-structured epidermis in CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber-treated group. Based on our findings, the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber with excellent antimicrobial properties is highly suitable for wound healing and skin tissue regeneration applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin tissue has been regarded as the largest and one of the most important organs of the body [1], which plays a vital role in protecting the body against environmental threats. Actually, intact skin is the first barrier that preserves internal tissues from the penetration of micro-organisms [2]. Therefore, damaged skin must be repaired rapidly to prevent overmuch blood loss and microbial infections [3]. The wound generally needs to be covered by a dressing to speed up the restoration of skin integrity. Over the past few decades, progression in the tissue engineering field has introduced new trends in composite wound dressing as a promising substitute for healing skin damage [4]. A desirable dressing should have these properties: protect the wound against microbial infections, provide a moist environment at the wound site, absorb excess exudates of the wound, and create a good gas exchange level. Additionally, it is important to apply nontoxic, non-allergic, and biocompatible materials for skin injuries [5,6]. Biopolymers like chitosan, sodium alginate, collagen, and hyaluronic acid are the most popular dressing materials for skin tissue engineering [7,8], which have been used in different forms, such as solid sheets/sponges [9], fibers [10], and hydrogels [11].

Chitosan is a unique biopolymer consisting of D-glucosamine and N-acetyl D-glucosamine, which can be achieved by the partial deacetylation of chitin [12]. In the wound healing process, chitosan can act as a hemostatic [13] and antibacterial agent [14] in the first and second phases of healing, respectively. The formation of granulation tissue is a critical stage in wound healing; research has shown that chitosan has a significant role at this stage by regulating the activity of inflammatory cells, fibroblast proliferation, and collagen deposition [15]. Chitosan nanofiber mats produced by the electrospinning technique have a wide range of applications. However, the high viscosity of pure chitosan complicates its electrospinning possibility [16], so in this study, we use silk fibroin as the second polymer, which favors the electrospinning of chitosan. Silk fibroin is a natural protein extracted from the domesticated Bombyx mori, which exhibits strong potential for many applications. Valuable features of this natural biopolymer include its elasticity, flexibility, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [17,18], which enabled it to be extensively used in biomedical fields such as bone tissue regeneration and wound healing [19,20]. Research on the wound healing capacity of SF-based biomaterials exhibited excellent properties such as good cell adhesion [21] and fibroblast proliferation. Through progression in the nanoscience field, new antimicrobial agents have been represented in the nanoscale, especially carbon nanomaterials owing to their unique physicochemical properties [22]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fascinating members of carbon-based nanomaterials with small sizes (mainly <10 nm) that have been classified as 0D nanostructures. These materials have inspired a great interest in different research fields due to their chemical stability, low toxicity, and easy synthesis methods with various low-cost materials [23,24]. Previous studies have demonstrated the bactericidal activity of CQDs [25,26]. In our work, we consider this property to improve the antibacterial efficiency of the designed dressing. Moreover, the stabilization of nanofibers with the cross-linking agents is along with increasing surface hydrophobicity [27], while the hydrophilic nature of CQDs can be potentially effective for surface hydrophilicity enhancement [28].

Previous investigations have proved that bioactive materials have beneficial effects on the wound microenvironment toward promoting wound healing [29]. Tricalcium phosphate is an inorganic material with excellent biodegradability and bioactivity features that result in extensive applications in the tissue engineering field [30]. On the other hand, wound healing is a calcium-mediated process [31,32]. CaP-NPs has been known to have a key role in the normal homeostasis of skin and act as a modulator for the proliferation and differentiation of keratinocyte [33]. Previous Studies take some approaches toward applying CaP-NPs for wound healing, such as intravenous or topical administration of CaP-NPs [34] and nanoparticle-coated nanofibers [35]; however, incorporating calcium phosphate into scaffolds can enhance their bioactivity for facilitating wound healing.

To the best of our knowledge, there is not any scientific report about the properties of silk fibroin/chitosan blended nanofibers incorporated with N-CQDs/α-TCP nanoparticles. Also, the use of this type of nanofibrous scaffold as a wound dressing has not been reported so far. Hence in this research, N-CQDs/α-TCP nanoparticles were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal procedure and were further characterized. Afterward, the electrospinning method was applied to fabricate the CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP mat. The physicochemical properties of the Prepared dressing were studied using several tools. In vitro cell studies, including MTT assay and scratch test, were carried out to investigate the toxicity and proliferation capacity of nanofibers. At the in vivo stage, full-thickness wounds were created on rat models, and as-prepared scaffolds were used as the treatment agents to evaluate the wound healing efficacy of these synthesized nanofibers.

In this research, fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and 0.25 % Trypsin-EDTA were purchased from gibco, 3-(4,5 dimethyl thiazol- 2-yl)-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide, Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), Trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), and chitosan medium molecular weight were obtained from Sigma- Aldrich.

Human fetal dermal fibroblast (HFF) cell line was obtained from Yazd Reproductive Sciences Institute and cultured in high-glucose DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % Penicillin/streptomycin in 75 cm2 cell culture flasks with incubation at 37 ∘C and 5 % CO2.

In this study, healthy male Wistar rats weighing 250–270 g were housed individually in standard conditions (standard laboratory rat food and drinkable water, 12 h light/dark cycle, and 21–25 °C temperature) in Yazd Reproductive Sciences Institute, Yazd, Iran.
Bacterial strains, including Escherichia (E. coli) ATCC 25922 and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) ATCC 25923, were purchased from Tissa Gene Zist Company.

The N-doped CQDs were synthesized based on a hydrothermal method described in our previous work [36]. Briefly, 2 g di-Ammonium hydrogen citrate was dispersed in 75 mL distilled water with constant stirring at 25 ∘C. Then, this solution was transferred into a hydrothermal bomb and kept at 150 ∘C for 18 h to achieve a 26.6 mg/mL CQDs solution. To obtain a uniform solution, bath sonication was carried out for 10 min. Afterward, 40 mg of Tricalcium phosphate was added to 20 mL of prepared CQDs (10 mg/mL) under constant stirring for 2 h to obtain 12 mg/mL CQDs/α-TCP nanohybrid. Then the mixture was sonicated for 10 min in a bath sonicator and subsequently probing sonicator for 10 and 20 min, respectively. At last, the prepared solutions were kept at −20 ∘C overnight and then freeze-dried. The synthesized nanomaterials were stored at 4 °C until used.

Firstly, chitosan solution was prepared in TFA at a concentration of 4 % (w/v) with constant stirring for 24 h at room temperature. Similarly, SF dissolved in TFA for 1 h to yield 7 % (w/v) SF solution. The CS/SF blend solution in a volume ratio of (70:30) was prepared by mixing CS and SF stock solutions and stirred for 1 h to achieve a uniform solution. To obtain the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers, 5 % (w/v) of the N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite (as the optimal concentration) was added to the mixture under constant stirring for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous solution. Afterward, a 5 mL syringe was filled with the prepared solution and fitted to a 17-gauge needle tip, and then the syringe was mounted on the syringe pump. The electrospinning process was carried out in the optimum condition of 0.7 mL/h feed rate, a voltage of 21 Kv, 15 cm distance between syringe tip and collector, and 27 °C temperature.
The best electrospun mat obtained in this step was selected for the following investigations.

To stabilize the prepared mats (CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs) against disintegration in water, chemical treatment was applied through steaming nanofibers with 3 % glutaraldehyde aqueous solution in a desiccator for 12 h. Then, treated mats were washed with PBS and kept at room temperature overnight.

The crystalline nature of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP nanomaterials was investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fourier transform spectroscopy (FT-IR) was applied to determine the main functional groups of the synthesized nanoparticles and nanofibers. The surface morphology of the nanostructures was investigated using TESCAN Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were performed on a Philips EM208S 100KV Transmission Electron Microscope to discover the morphology of the N-CQD/α-TCP nanocomposite.
Contact angle measurements of the electrospun mats were performed by the Jikan CAG-20 instrument. The experiment was repeated 3 times for each sample and average values were reported.

The mechanical properties of the mats were evaluated with a universal testing instrument using a 50 N load cell. All nanofibers samples were cut to the size of 40 mm × 10 mm, and a cross-head speed of 2 mm min−1 was applied for all the specimens tested. Three specimens were tested for each scaffold sample to evaluate average values and standard deviation.

The swelling test was carried out for prepared nanofibrous mats (CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP NFs) to determine the water absorption ability of each sample. First, the two types of scaffolds were weighed and then immersed in PBS for 24 h at 37 °C. At the predetermined time, each specimen was removed from PBS, and after discarding excess PBS, the secondary weight of the samples was measured.

The water uptake capacity of the scaffolds was calculated corresponding to the following formula:
Swelling rate
%
=
W 1
−
W 2
W 1
× 100
The average values with the standard deviation of three samples of each scaffold were reported and the comparison between the two groups was performed using the t-test method.
The biodegradability properties of optimized CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP nanofibers were determined by the following protocol:

At first, 1 cm × 1 cm of the pre-weighed cross-linked samples were immersed in 10 mL PBS (PH:7.4) and incubated at 37 °C. The degradability ratio of the mats was evaluated for ten days, and at regular intervals of 24 h, the scaffolds were removed from the PBS solution, dried, and weighed. The biodegradation rate was quantified using the following expression:
Degradation
%
=
W 1
−
W 2
W 1
× 100

The antibacterial activities of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/α-TCP, CS/N-CQDs/α-TCP, and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP materials were evaluated using the turbidimetric procedure. Bacterial species, including Escherichia (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), were grown in a Muller-Hinton broth medium with incubation at 37 °C for 18 h. To standardize the experiment, 0.5 MacFarland solution was made using sterile distilled water and considered micro-organisms. The optical density (OD600) of the solution was adjusted to 0.11.

The (MIC) values were investigated by a broth micro-dilution assay. First, all wells of a sterile microtiter plate were filled with 100 μL of sterile M-H broth medium, then 100 μL of each sample was added to the first well of each row, and dilution was performed until the 10th well followed by inoculation with 5 μL of 0.5 MacFarland solution. Well, containing microbial suspension was considered as the positive control, and the negative control included culture medium only. For each bacteria test was performed on a separate plate. Then plates were placed in a shaker incubator for 24 h at 37 °C. Afterward, the turbidity of each well was measured at 600 nm. The minimum concentration of each sample that inhibited the growth of bacteria was considered to be MIC and expressed in mg/mL. Experiments were performed in triplicate and the data were analyzed using GraphPad Prism software version 7 and two-way ANOVA, Sidak's multiple comparisons test.

The cytotoxicity of the prepared CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP nanofibers was measured using the MTT assay under ISO 10993-5 guidelines [37]. First, sterilized nanofibers were incubated in the culture medium (DMEM+1 % Penicillin/streptomycin) for 24 h, and the extractions were used for the MTT test. Human foreskin fibroblast Cells (HFF) were cultured in a 24-well tissue culture plate (15 × 103 cells/well) and were supplied with DMEM media (supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % Penicillin/streptomycin). In the control group, cells were treated with a culture medium without nanofibers. Then the plate was maintained at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 humidified incubator for 24, 48, and 72 h. At the time point out of 24, 48, or 72 h, the medium was recharged with 300 μL of fresh medium containing 5 mg/mL MTT solution and kept in the incubator for 4 h. In the end, the medium was removed, and 300 μL DMSO was added to each well. Then after 20 min, the optical density was measured at 570 nm using an ELISA plate reader. The viability of wells containing CS/SF and CS/SF/CQD-α-TCP nanofibers was calculated as the control-based percentage with the following formula:
Cell viability
%
=
OD treated
OD
control
× 100
where OD treated and OD control represent the optical density of treated and untreated cells, respectively. For each sample, tests were carried out in triplicate, and the average values with standard deviation of each group were also calculated and compared using the two-way ANOVA Turkey's multiple comparison test by Graph Pad Prism software version 7.

A scratch test was performed to evaluate the proliferation and migration effect of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite toward HFF fibroblast cells. Sample preparation was carried out according to ISO 10993-12 standard test sample preparation. The wound dressing was sterilized and then immersed in the culture medium (DMEM +1 % Penicillin/streptomycin) for 24 h to obtain the extract. Then, Cells were cultured into a 24-well cell culture plate with incubation (37 °C, 5 % CO2) to achieve a confluent cell monolayer. Afterward, a sterile 100 μL pipette tip was used to create a linear wound at the center of each well. Then, wells were washed with PBS to remove all Cellular debris, and the sample (as a therapeutic candidate) was added to each well. In the Control group, the medium (DMEM+FBS) was poured into the wound area to compare the treated group with the control. The plate was incubated in the condition of 37 °C temperature and 5 % CO2, and migration was followed by taking images with an Olympus IX2-SL microscope every day.

The wound surface area at the start of the incubation (S0) and 24 h later (S24) was photographed by phase-contrast Olympus IX2-SL microscope, and the wound closure was measured using ImageJ software. The wound healing percentage was calculated using the following equation:
Wound closure
%
=
S 24
S
0
× 100
Wound healing rate in control and experimental groups was statistically compared using the t-test. The displayed data is the mean and standard deviation of three independent trials.

In vivo wound healing evaluation was done to determine the impact of CS/SF/CQD-α-TCP nanofiber on the progression of the wound healing process, using a full-thickness excisional wound model. Before the surgery, animals were weighed and anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of ketamine/xylazine (100/10 mg kg_1). Then, the dorsal region of the rats was shaved, and a shear wound of 10 × 10 mm2 was created. The animals were randomly divided into four groups, and each group included four rats: (1) CS/SF nanofiber treated rats, (2) CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP nanofiber treated rats, (3) phenytoin treated rats (as a positive control group), and (4) non-treated rats. Each Then, wound dressings were applied just after wound surgery, and wounds in all groups were photographed at 0, 3, 6, 9, and 12 days after wound surgery. The wound area was measured by Image J software, and statistical analysis was done using the two-way ANOVA Turkey's multiple comparison test. The percentage of wound healing on the specific days of the experiment in comparison to the 0-day wound was calculated using the following equation:
Wound healing
%
=
Wound area
specific
day
− Wound area
first
day
Wound area
first
day
× 100

For the historical study, wound tissues were excised and then fixed in 4 % buffered paraformaldehyde (v/v) for several days. Then tissues were embedded in paraffin, and tissue blocks were cut to 5 μm in thickness as a standard method. Subsequently, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was carried out to evaluate the re-epithelialization rate in each group of experiments.

FTIR analysis was applied to determine the chemical bonds of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP. As shown in Fig. 1A, a broad peak around 3500 cm_1 was observed, which refers to the stretching vibration of NH and OH from amino and hydroxyl groups, while 1198 cm_1 and 1416 cm_1 peaks signify CN and OH (bending vibration) bonds, respectively. Peaks around 1710 cm_1 and 1577 cm_1 can attribute to CO and CN, respectively. About TCP powder, sharp peaks at 566 and 603 cm−1 correspond to the bending modes of PO4 −3, and peaks at 962 and 1092 cm_1 indicate stretching modes of PO4 −3. In the case of the N-CQD/α-TCP composite, Peaks at 602, 563, and 1039 cm_1 are attributed to PO4 −3. Peaks around 1409, 1672, 1710, 2971, and 3258 cm_1 refer to the N-CQDs. These results indicate the plausible incorporation of N-CQDs and α-TCP NPs.

XRD spectrum of pure α-TCP, N-CQDs, and N-CQDs/α-TCP nanohybrid are shown in Fig. 1B. A Single broad diffraction peak around 24° could be attributed to the amorphous nature of carbon-based materials [38]. The as-received TCP indicated the typical X-ray pattern, and the N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite exhibited similar characteristic peaks as TCP without new diffraction peaks, except that the peak at 22° became sharper. Since graphite has a characteristic peak at this angle [39], this slight change could refer to the hybridization of N-CQDs with TCP.

The morphology of nanomaterials was illustrated by SEM and TEM imaging. Typical TEM images of N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite powder are shown in Fig. 1C. The SEM images of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs are displayed in Figs. 2A and 3A , respectively.

Elemental mapping was applied to explore the composition of prepared samples. The EDX spectra of the N-CQDs confirmed the presence of Nitrogen, Carbon, and oxygen with atomic percentages of 31.8, 44.79, and 23.41 %, respectively (Fig. 2, B-F), and in the case of N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs, the presence of Nitrogen, Oxygen, Calcium, Phosphor, and Carbon with an abundance of 4.25, 48.87, 12.97, 11.02 and 22.9 % verified (Fig. 3, B-H). These findings certify that considered elements were evenly distributed in N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs.

Fig. 4 represents typical FTIR spectra of pure chitosan, pure silk fibroin, chitosan/silk fibroin nanofiber, and chitosan/silk fibroin/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber. The infrared spectrum of pure chitosan showed bands at 1025, 1375, 1596, and 1662 cm−1, which are assignable to COC stretching vibrations, CC stretching, amide II (CN, NH) and amide I (CO) groups, respectively. Peaks located at 2886 and 3351 cm−1 are attributed to CH (stretching vibrations) and NH or OH (stretch vibrations of the hydroxyl), respectively. Pure silk fibroin FTIR pattern illustrates intense absorption peaks at 1621, 1514, and 1230 cm−1, corresponding to amide I, II, and III bands, respectively. A broad peak at 3278 cm−1 is assignable to NH stretching of amid A groups. In the case of CS/SF nanofiber, characteristic bonds from both chitosan and silk fibroin are visible. Our results are similar to other works [40,41]. When N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs were incorporated into CS/SF nanofiber, new peaks were observed at 607 and 1075 cm−1 attributed to the PO4 −3 groups of α-TCP. Characteristic peaks of N-doped CQDs (OH, NH, and CN) merge with CS/SF bonds in the composite. These results confirm the successful fabrication of CQDs/α-TCP NPs embedded CS/SF nanofiber.

The morphology of the CS/SF/N-CQDs-α-TCP nanofibers was monitored by a scanning electron microscope [SEM] (TESCAN VEGA3). Fig. 5 shows the CS/SF/N-CQDs-α-TCP nanofiber micrograph before and after chemical treatment. Non-cross linked mat has a ranging diameter from 100 nm to 570 nm with an average diameter of 515 ± 21 nm. As can be observed from the microscopic images, the nanofibers have a bead-free appearance, and a ribbon-like shape was observed at some points. In the case of cross-linked nanofibers, the slight increase in fiber diameter can be assigned to glutaraldehyde vapor treatment. The presence of Carbon, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Phosphor, and Calcium elements with an abundance of 45.34, 38.82, 10.4, 2.51, and 2.92 % was approved by EDS spectrum and EDX mapping (Fig. 5, D-I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle and water uptake capacity
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the water contact angle (WCA) measurement are shown in Fig. 6 . As shown in Fig. 6A, chitosan/silk fibroin nanofibers had a contact angle of 51.25° ± 1.5. When N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs were incorporated into the nanofibers, the water contact angle decreased to 38.68° ± 2.7 (Fig. 6B), revealing N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs' effect on surface hydrophilicity. Han et al. found that CDs could decrease the WCA of chitosan nanocomposite [42]. Yusof et al. reported an 11 % reduction in WCA when TCP particles changed from 0.75 to 1 Wt% in PLLA/CMS/β-TCP nanofibers [43]. Fig. 6D shows the water uptake ability of CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP nanofibrous mats. The CS/SF/CQDs/α-TCP scaffold showed higher water absorbance (357.3 ± 15.03 %) compared to CS/SF scaffold (342.3 ± 19.33 %), Although the differences were statistically no significant.

In-vitro biodegradability of cross-linked CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs-α-TCP nanofibers were studied, and the results are shown as a curve in Fig. 6E. Our observation reveals that CS/SF blended nanofiber had a degradability of 54.6 ± 5.5 % (remaining mass), which is reasonable due to the presence of Functional hydrophilic OH−, NH2, and COOH groups of chitosan and silk fibroin polymers. While by adding N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs, the degradation speed is decreased to 63.2 ± 5.7 % (remaining mass).

In a recent study, Goodarzi et al. developed PCL/PHBV core-shell nanofibers incorporating different amounts of graphene oxide (GO) and calcium phosphate (CP). The tensile analysis demonstrated that the mechanical properties improved when GO and CP concentrations were increased. Notably, at the same GO concentration, higher content of CP revealed a more desirable tensile behavior [47].

Management of microbial infection during the wound healing process is one of the most critical issues, especially in the case of chronic lesions like diabetic foot ulcers, so applying an antibacterial dressing can efficiently shorten the inflammatory phase and consequently reduce the healing period. In this study, we evaluated the antibacterial activity of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/α-TCP, CS/N-CQDs/α-TCP, and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP materials against S. aureus and E. coli (the most common species cause wound infection), using MIC test.

The MIC results are presented in Fig. 8 . Prepared N-CQDs indicated MIC values of 5.28 ± 0.3 mg/mL and 7.37 ± 0.33 mg/mL for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. With the addition of TCP to CQDs, the MIC decreased for both species, which manifests the inhibitory effect of TCP; however, S. aureus showed more susceptibility to N-CQDs/α-TCP sample (3.53 ± 0.28 mg/mL Vs. 4.3 ± 0.22 mg/mL). For both E. coli and S. aureus, CS/N-CQDs/α-TCP exhibited significantly lower MIC values than N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP materials (1.58 ± 0.4 mg/mL for E. coli compared to 1.76 ± 0.12 mg/mL for S. aureus). According to previous studies, the great antibacterial property of Chitosan could be attributed to its polycationic structure, which electrostatically reacts with the anionic groups of the bacterial cell surface, which cause alteration in cell permeability and physiological activities of the bacteria and consequently leads to death of the bacterial cell [48]. In the case of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP, despite the higher inhibitory activity against both species, no significant differences were observed compared to the CS/N-CQDs/α-TCP sample.

Cytotoxicity assay of electrospun mats was performed as a standard preclinical test toward fibroblast cells (HFF), as shown in Fig. 9A. Our results reveal that after 24 h incubation, in comparison to the non-treated group, CS/SF NFs treated group had no toxicity effect on HFF cells, and the cell viability of CS/SF NFs (112.8 ± 4.5 %) was higher than the control (103.2 ± 5.49 %), which indicates that CS/SF NFs can promote cell proliferation. This finding is in concordance with other similar works [49]. The CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs group was far superior to the control and CS/SF treated group in viability rate (120.9 ± 4.02 %). Also, by 48 and 72 h of incubation, the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs group showed the highest absorbance values compared to the two other groups, which, based on similar studies, could be attributed to the proliferation activity of CQDs and TCP NPs. K. Kawai et al. synthesized pH-sensitive calcium-based nanoparticles (CNPs), and in-Vitro experiments were performed on fibroblast cells to evaluate the proliferation effect of prepared NPs. Bromodeoxyuridine (BrdU) labeling showed that in the presence of the highest CNP concentration, the proliferation rate was increased near 25 %. Through wound healing assay, they discovered that the healing process was more rapid in CNP-treated mice [34]. Fig. 9B, C, and D represent the morphology and shape of HFF cells after 72 h incubation in the presence of the CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NCs, indicating that HFF cells preserved their fibroblast shape after treatment with CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NCs.

The results of the MTT assay revealed that all prepared scaffolds were not only toxic for fibroblast cells but also could promote cell proliferation. An in-vitro scratch assay was conducted in the presence of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP mats to evaluate the therapeutic effect of this nanocomposite. As seen in Fig. 10B, in the treatment group, there was nearly 73.23 ± 2.71 % of fibroblast cell migration into the wound site, which compared to the control group with only 26.86 ± 5.41 % of migration, CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP composite could effectively induce cell growth and showed good healing properties. Besides the proliferative effect of Chitosan, several studies have demonstrated that CQDs have a significant role in cell migration and wound healing. In a study, P. Li et al. synthesized CQDs by pyrolysis of lysine and arginine, and at the in-vitro scale, CQDs promoted the growth of NIH 3 T3 fibroblast cells. The in-vivo wound healing assay proved that prepared CQDs could inhibit bacterial infection on the wound surface and accelerate wound healing [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing study and histopathologic evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the wound healing performance of the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP dressing on a rat model. A 1cm2 full-thickness wound was formed on the back of each rat, and subsequently, CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP scaffolds were applied as the therapeutic agents, phenytoin was used as the positive control, and the blank group included no treatment rats. The macroscopic appearance and the rate of wound closure at 0,3,6,9 and 12 days after surgery are represented in Fig. 11A and B, respectively. A gradual wound healing was observed in all groups, but the statistical data showed that the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP scaffold induced the strongest effect on wound closure compared to other groups. On the 3rd day, CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP treated rats showed significant wound closure by about 58.34 ± 2.57 %, while CS/SF, phenytoin, and blank groups reached lower closure rates (33.29 ± 2.96 %, 24.3 ± 2.84 %, and 23.08 ± 2.2 %, respectively). This considerable difference between CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP treated group, and the other three groups are also visible on 6, 9, and 12 days post-injury. There was no obvious difference in wound closure percentage between the blank and phenytoin group (85.02 ± 1.55 % and 86.33 ± 0.73 %, respectively) on day 12, and the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP group with 96.73 ± 1.25 % healing rate was followed by CS/SF group (89.51 ± 1.67 %).

H&E staining was performed to evaluate the wound healing effect of the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP scaffold from the histological perspective (Fig. 12 ). On day six postoperation, a large amount of inflammatory cell infiltration was found in all groups, which was more evident in the blank group than in the other three groups. Meanwhile, CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs treated group induced maximum angiogenesis and connective tissue fibers reconstruction compared to the others. Most importantly, it is clear from the results that the regenerated stratified epithelium tissues were more intact and thicker in the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers treated group than in the other groups. On day 12 of the healing period, inflammatory cell infiltration was reduced in all groups, especially in the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers treated group. Also, normal vascularity and regular epidermal structure were found in this group. In CS/SF and CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs treated groups, the connection between epidermis and connective tissue was formed as the papillary dermis, which this structure was more obvious in the latter. In sharp contrast, in the blank group, the epithelium is still thin and without papillary dermis structures.

Recently, great progress has been made in developing antibacterial wound dressings. In this study, a chitosan-silk fibroin complex electrospun scaffold incorporating N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs was successfully prepared via the electrospinning method to accelerate wound healing. Chitosan, due to its unique macromolecular structure and physiochemical characteristics, obtained specific attention in many research areas, especially in the medical and pharmaceutical fields [51]. Chitosan has the potential to be processed easily into different forms such as hydrogels, sponges, microparticles and nanoparticles, membranes, and fibers that makes extensive use of this material in pharmaceutical formulation, gene and drug delivery, regenerative medicine, biosensors, and biodevices [52].

Previous studies have demonstrated that chitosan with biochemical activities such as fibroblast activation, hemostatic effect, cell migration, and antibacterial activity could promote wound healing [53–55]. The hemostatic activity of chitosan is associated with its positively charged surface, which reacts with negatively charged platelet and erythrocyte, leading to platelet and erythrocyte aggregation. Moreover, a study in 1992 demonstrated that chitosan could induce extending hemostasis by inhibiting fibrinolysis during the hemostatic phase [56]. Moreover, the degradation of chitosan in the human body by lysozymes produces harmless products that can easily metabolize and makes chitosan an excellent biocompatible material to manufacture appropriate scaffolds for tissue regeneration applications [57]. Additionally, active functional groups like hydroxyl carboxyl and amino groups give a flexible structure to chitosan to undergo chemical modification [58].

Furthermore, a number of studies proved that a 3D matrix of chitosan obtained by electrospinning procedure could mimic natural ECM for cell adhesion and proliferation [59,60]. In the field of skin tissue regeneration, the mechanical behavior of dressings is critical for their applications [61]. In our work, we applied silk fibroin as a co-polymer to overcome the low spinnability of chitosan and enhance the mechanical properties of nanofibers [62,63]. Recently, Carbon-based nanomaterials have been widely used as a promising agent for improving the antimicrobial and mechanical properties of nanofibrous scaffolds [64]. The endogenous ROS induction and cell membrane disruption, inspired by the positive charge of CQDs, are two possible mechanisms of the antibacterial activity of CQDs [65,66]. Herein we chose a bottom-up procedure to prepare N-CQDs by a simple hydrothermal method. Afterward, α-TCP was hybridized with N-CQDs to enhance the bioactivity and antibacterial efficiency of the nanocomposite. XRD and FTIR analysis certified the complexation between N-CQD and α-TCP. Then N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs embedded CS/SF nanofibers were fabricated at 5 % (w/v) of nanoparticles with an average fiber diameter of 515 ± 21 nm. SEM images confirmed well-arranged nanofibers. EDX and elemental mapping confirmed the well distribution of N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs in nanofibers.

Mechanical investigation showed that after incorporating N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs to nanofibers, the elastic modulus increased significantly which can refer to functionalized OH groups of CQDs that reacts with chitosan surface and improve the hydrogen bonding.

A hydrophilic surface is an important aspect of wound dressings that induces better nutrition absorption and cell growth, leading to rapid wound healing [67]. We measured the water contact angle of as-spun nanofibers to evaluate the effect of N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs on the hydrophilicity of the CS/SF scaffold. The results revealed that, after N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs incorporation, surface hydrophilicity improved. This reduction of contact angle could attribute to the presence of hydrophilic groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, on the surface of CQDs. In addition, negatively charged phosphate (PO4 3−) attracts H+ ions which help in better wettability [68]. Liang et al. developed a ZnO QDs@GO-CS hydrogel, composing zinc oxide quantum dots, graphene oxide sheets, and chitosan hydrogel, an antibacterial nanocomposite, to enhance wound healing. WCA measurement revealed that increasing the amount of ZnO QDs@GO could efficiently improve the hydrophilicity of ZnO QDs@GO-CS hydrogel, and this moist environment combined with antibacterial properties of hydrogel exhibits an excellent wound healing performance [69]. Wound exudate management is another critical issue during the wound-healing process to prevent microbial infections. In this study, CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP fibrous mats showed higher water uptake capacity than CS/SF mats due to superior surface hydrophilicity suggesting higher wound exudate absorption.

In accordance with the current research, the results of the in vitro cytotoxicity assessment revealed that cell proliferation was significantly higher in CS/SF/N-CQD/α-TCP NFs treated group compared with the control and CS/SF NFs treated group. It has been demonstrated that the Ca ion can increase the proliferation of fibroblast cells [31]; also, several studies have verified the ability of CQDs to promote mammalian cell proliferation and migration [36,50]. Shafiei et al. investigated the proliferation rate of hBFPSCs cells in the presence of PCL/PVA nanofibrous mats, incorporating TCP and CDs materials. The MTT assay results showed the synergistic effect of TCP and CDs could induce better cell proliferation [70].

We also carried out in vivo wound healing test to examine the practicality of the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP scaffold for clinical application. Plenty of studies have demonstrated the role of Chitosan in accelerating wound healing. Actually, besides the antibacterial and hemostatic activity, Chitosan can increase the secretion of the TGF-β, PDGF, and IL-1 from macrophages which induce macrophage migration, fibroblast migration and proliferation, and angiogenesis stimulation, respectively, and eventually promote the growth of granulation tissue which refill wound site and promote epidermal regeneration [56,71]. Moreover, the role of Ca2+ in wound healing was determined in previous studies [35]. S.H. Jeong et al. reported that Ca2+ could increase the level of fibroblast cells in the dermis by accelerating the expression of TGF and EGF through the SMAD signaling pathway [72]. It can also be a plausible description of the role of TCP in the expedition of wound healing. Altogether the aforementioned data verified that antibacterial CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP dressing has a promising application in skin tissue regeneration.

In summary, we successfully fabricated a novel CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibrous scaffold by the electrospinning method as a potential wound dressing. First, N-CQDs and N-CQDs/α-TCP nanoparticles were synthesized and then were well characterized. The SEM analysis confirmed the uniform and bead-free morphology of the nanofibrous mats. Water contact angle measurement revealed that incorporating N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs into chitosan/silk fibroin nanofibrous mats increased the hydrophilicity of the scaffold. Also, the tensile test showed CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofibers exhibited appropriate Young's modulus and tensile strength. The swelling and biodegradability studies further proved that the N-CQDs/α-TCP NPs improved the exudate uptake capacity and non-degradability of the nanofibrous composite. In addition, the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP showed higher antibacterial performance than other experimental groups. The MIC values of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP were found to be 1.45 ± 0.26 and 1.59 ± 0.12 mg/mL for S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Through In-vitro cell cytotoxicity assessment, we found that CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanofiber not only had no toxicity effect on HFF cells but also visible cell proliferation was observed after 3 days of cell culturing.

Additionally, in-vitro scratch assay revealed that 73.23 ± 2.71 % of cell migration occurred over 24 h in the presence of CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs. In vivo wound healing investigation demonstrated that CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP NFs treated group exhibited better angiogenesis and re-epithelialization with less inflammatory cell infiltration than other groups, and during the 12-day study period, 96.73 ± 1.25 % of the wound closure was recorded. Based on our findings, CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite is a promising dressing for skin wound healing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: The study was ethically approved by the Ethics Committee of Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences, Yazd, Iran. (Approval number IR.SSU.MEDICINE.REC.1399.006).
The authors consent to publishing all details and images for this manuscript.
This study was supported by a grant from the student research committee at Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences, Yazd, Iran.

ND participated in the design of the study, performing the study, acquisition of data, and writing the article. FY and MP were involved in the conception and design of the study. FH and NG were involved in supervising and writing the article. FS and FM have participated in the acquisition of data from animal experiments and histological analysis. SMN has participated in the statistical analysis, data interpretation, and manuscript revision. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.
